title: Remote Control Car
subtitle: ASME * RC Derby 2026
blurb: Designed and iterated a remote-controlled car using a servo-driven steering system through multiple linkage configurations; contributed to chassis layout and component integration after designing in OnShape. Placed 3rd of 6 teams as the only freshman competitors in the UCF ASME 2026 RC Derby.
description: This project was done for the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) student chapter at the University of Central Florida, specifically for their RC Derby, a competition tasking teams with building a remote-controlled car under size, battery, and component restrictions, then racing it around a small track for time.

The initial design consisted almost entirely of 3D-printed parts for ease of construction and weight. The components would rest on a flat plate, with the electronics (battery, ESC, microcontroller / receiver) in the center, motors in the rear, and a servo-steering system in the front. The steering system was originally a complicated mechanism involving several shafts and gears, along with other concepts that we experimented with. We eventually landed on the steering 'rod' design, where a servo pushes a rigid arm left or right to then rotate a set of wheels in that direction. The motors and electrical components remained unchanged, and did not need much innovation.

Overall, the car was serviceable for how quickly it came together. Since most of the legwork was refining the steering, and considering we all had little experience with engineering as a whole during this project, I'm glad it worked well enough on race day. Still, the car was not without its issues— the joints that the wheels rested on had some difficulty turning smoothly, and our turning radius suffered as a result. The front wheels often slid instead of rolling, which hurt our speed. This was made more difficult due to the fact that the remote control we were given had faulty controls. Because we received the parts on race day, cable management also proved to be something that interfered with performance. Nevertheless, we completed the loop several times and finished 3rd place out of 6 competing teams- the only freshmen participating. While we could have done better on race day, I am more satisfied with my growth as a result of this project. I became more comfortable with OnShape and the engineering design process, specifically in attempting to model as much as I could on my own. While it was difficult to model the wheel mounts and pins to be the perfect size, the trial and error was key to my learning. My teammates helped me better understand soldering and the order in which the electronics operated. Most importantly, this project pushed me to be a more effective leader, as I would often close group meetings with reminders, deadlines, and task assignments for each member.

images.caption: The completed car with all its systems in place.

A more finalized version of the car's steering system. The final steering system closely resembles this mockup.

Various pins and collars for different uses across the car.

title: Autonomous Submarine
subtitle: Engineering Concepts and Methods * Great Naval Orange Race 2026
blurb: Designed and iterated propulsion and guidance systems for a fully autonomous submarine as part of UCF's Great Naval Orange Race 2026; led multiple redesigns of the rudder, propeller shroud, and dual-propeller steering mechanism in OnShape. Contributed to hull construction and electronics integration, including waterproofing and wiring of onboard components.
description: This project was done for the Lab portion of EGS 1007C 'Engineering Concepts and Methods,' specifically the 2026 Great Navel Orange Race. The assignment tasks students in groups of 4-5 with constructing a fully autonomous aquatic vehicle capable of transporting a navel orange across UCF's Reflection Pond in a semicircle, with a long list of restrictions and regulations regarding the final build.

Originally, we had bounced around ideas of an airboat or a speedboat, eventually settling on a submarine after feeling that we were capable of the challenge. My original sketch detailed our preliminary ideas- we would completely waterproof the hull using a sealant, placing the orange within the detachable nose cone of the submarine and harboring the electronics within the center of the vessel. A steering and propulsion system sat in the rear of the sub, although it was vague in detail since we had yet to define it completely. It included a ballast system involving the use of a servo or motor to pull or push the plunger of a large syringe to increase or decrease the mass of the sub for floating and sinking. The hull itself was intended to be completely modeled in CAD and 3D-printed with a low infill to counteract the negatively-buoyant PLA. As time passed and the physical work began, our team realized we had designed a vehicle that was too complex for the budget and part restrictions, not to mention the time it would take for us to troubleshoot these systems on limited hardware. Later sketches reflected this as we downsized the operation greatly- we shrank the physical size by a third, removed the ballast system entirely, and designed a less sophisticated system of electrical installation and orange stowage. Because I was tasked with propulsion and guidance, most of my sketches involved several revamps of the rudder, propeller, propeller shroud, and the steering mechanism. After countless redesigns, the final build was massively simplified to reflect the aforementioned limitations. Instead of a detachable nose cone, the cylindrical hull and nose were attached, with the rear of the submarine (now lacking any shroud or rudder) being a small disk with a long 'platform' in its center that housed the electronics, which sat in a waterproofed Tupperware container. Steering was to be accomplished with a new dual-propeller system, in which more power would be given to the port or starboard propeller to yaw the vessel in either direction. Because of this, the submarine had no way of correcting its roll or pitch, and instead had to rely on consistent buoyancy. Without a ballast, we determined the best method of sinking the submarine was to waterproof all electronics before filling the inside of the hull entirely with water.

The submarine failed on race day due to several concurrent issues. For one, we had consistent troubles fitting the Tupperware into the cylindrical hull, since the edges of the container were too wide for the circular opening. Consequently, the back of the sub sat awkwardly attached, leaving not only a gap but also pushing the rear of the sub up and at an angle. The propeller shafts were also experiencing difficulty staying attached to the motors for some unknown reason, however it most likely had to do with the prior mentioned Tupperware issue. Since the motors themselves were fairly cheap and the shafts were not properly fitted, there was little to no propulsion once the submarine was let go.

I believe our greatest failure was communication in the first month of the project. When we did meet, we were incredibly focused and prepared to work, however we fell directionless not long after since we struggled to follow-up with each other. It was the tenacity and dedication in the last month and a half of the project that taught me the most in this project. We began to meet much more often, and we all took our individual assignments seriously. The project became our central focus and we were constantly designing, prototyping, failing, and redesigning. Thus, the project management we developed will stick with me forever. I became a more confident leader and I was more comfortable with assigning everyone to a task, as well as keeping up with progress after meetings subsided. As for technical skills, I became more confident and capable in my sketches as the project continued. It bled into my concurrent project, the Remote Control Car, where I began to make more detailed and useful sketches rather than rough scribbles. I had never used OnShape before this project, and taught myself out of necessity. I became more competent as the sketches became more defined and I became more confident in my ability to CAD what I had drawn on paper. In the last two weeks, when we began putting together the final build, I gained hands-on experience with the hardware and construction. I was tasked with completely rebuilding the Tupperware in the last couple of days, ensuring that it was secure to the hull plate and that the electronics it housed were operational and wired properly, along with being completely waterproof.

images.caption: The final 3D-printed hull.
From left to right, Tanish Pilli, Felix Lee, Robert Hagerty (EGS Lab Team Leader), Matthew Julian González, Jalol Nazirov. This picture was taken on Great Navel Orange Race 2026 Race Day.

First rough sketch showing the various planned components in little detail. In this original design, a rudder system and ballast system were proposed, both of which did not make it to the final build.
A more refined version of the initial sketch.
The original proposed rudder system. The later designs were offshoots of this concept.

The first proposed measurements for the diameter and length of the cylindrical component of the hull. These measurements ended up being much larger than we could account for and had to be changed, thus the title 'Blind' Measurements.
The original submarine design, featuring the old rudder shroud and 'door' concepts scaled down to the size of the palm of ones hand.
Working late at night into the morning of race day.
